explanation: Dead band is an insensitive region where input changes produce negligible output changes.
Band-pass combines high-pass (f > f_low) and low-pass (f < f_high) actions to select a frequency window.
An inverting summing amplifier outputs -(Rf/R1 V1 + Rf/R2 V2 + ...), a weighted sum of inputs.
Transient response describes how system reaches steady state.
Sensors measure actual system output for feedback.
-3dB corresponds to 10 log(0.5) = -3.01 dB; output power is half at this frequency.
Butterworth gives a maximally flat passband at the cost of a less steep transition; Chebyshev trades passband ripple for sharper roll-off.
Aliasing must be prevented before sampling, so an analog low-pass filter removes content above the Nyquist frequency ahead of the ADC.
Active gauge responds to strain; dummy responds to temperature only. Combined output cancels thermal error and doubles sensitivity.
At large strains the material deviates from linear R-strain relationship; plastic deformation and material change occur.
Over time, adhesive molecules relax slightly, causing apparent strain drift even though actual strain is constant.
Unequal expansion of the bonded metals causes the strip to curve as temperature changes.
Type-K (Chromel/Alumel) has ~41 µV/°C sensitivity, making it a popular general-purpose thermocouple.
Thermal time constant depends on sensor mass and thermal conductance between it and the medium.
The wiper voltage of a rotary potentiometer is proportional to shaft angle.
A measurement system has a sensing element, a signal-conditioning/processing element and a data presentation element.
Reproducibility (precision) is consistency of repeated measurements of the same quantity.
A consistent offset suggests a systematic error that affects all readings identically and can be corrected.
C = ε₀A/d shows C ∝ 1/d, so equal displacement changes produce unequal capacitance changes.
Incremental encoders track changes (delta position); absolute encoders directly encode position at each spot.
